messages.content:
I am making a c++ sdl based game engine, wrapping every functionality of SDL library into my own engine. Help me deal with problems and questions. 

The first one I have is this: I have a Point class based on SDL_Point, and a Renderer class based on SDL_Renderer, I got in a point that I have to convert from std::vector<Point> to std::vector<SDL_Point> for some rendering methods. So far I only have to convert the vector in renderer class, but I don't discard I may need it elsewhere. So I am doubtful if adding the converting method as a private method in Renderer or making it static and public in the Point class. I don't know if it will be correct to include an static method that is not directly related to a Point but to a vector of Points. How can I solve this dilema? Does this happen with standard libraries in C++ or C# too?

I read your suggestions and understood your logic, but utility functions inside a helper or utility namespace is something I don't want in the code, as it breaks the SOLID principles of object programming and hide away code and is also considered a code smell by many. I think I will go with the private method and if I ever need it in the future for a different class I would just put it as an static method inside Point, even thought it is not directly related I think it makes more sense than adding a namespace which I haven't already and I don't want to be adding in the future.

Your first suggestion I think is wrong, since passing a vector in a return would make a copy object from one to another when used, isn't better to use an out parameter like this?
void PointsToSDLVector(std::vector<SDL_Point>& outSDLPoints, std::vector<Point> points);
That was genius, thanks for the fixing, now I got it! So every time I need to use a vector it is better to pass it as refference modifying or not modifying it, right?

OK I got it thanks, my error was thinking that a void method didn't/shouldn't have to be const. Thanks for correcting me.
Now going back to the Renderer class, I need to do a FillCircle method, which is the one that will be the hardest to do, since it is not implemented in the original SDL library.
My idea is this, since I have a SDL_Point vector of all the points of the circle (using Midpoint circle algorithm), I am thinking on doing drawing lines between each upper point and its lower counterpart, is this idea correctly and efficient?
Why didn't you use the point vector to do it? Is this a better way or you forget?

I didn't use the point vector generated from the Midpoint circle algorithm in the pseudocode primarily because the most efficient way to fill a circle doesn't necessarily involve drawing lines between the set of discrete points on the circle's perimeter. Instead, drawing horizontal lines across the circle relies on a more direct calculation of the circle's coverage on each scanline, potentially avoiding the overhead of storing and iterating through points.

The Midpoint circle algorithm is excellent for drawing the outline of a circle since it leverages the symmetry of the circle to minimize the number of calculations required. When it comes to filling a circle, however, you want to minimize not just calculations but also drawing operations:

1. **Drawing Efficiency**: Drawing individual pixels or short lines between pairs of points is usually less efficient than drawing a horizontal line in a single operation. Graphics libraries and hardware are often optimized for drawing straight lines and continuous spans of pixels. By using `SDL_RenderDrawLine`, we leverage these optimizations.

2. **Calculation Overhead**: Storing and then processing all the points of the outline of the circle adds complexity and space overhead. If you only need to fill the circle, you don't necessarily need to know all the perimeter points ahead of time.

3. **Scanline Filling**: Using the scanline filling approach, you calculate and draw each horizontal line as you go, reducing the working set of data (you only store the min and max x-values for each y-value of the circle at any given time). This can be more cache-friendly and decrease memory usage, leading to performance gains.

If you have already implemented a function that calculates the outline of the circle and would like to reuse it, you could still fill the circle by using those points to guide the drawing of horizontal lines. However, this may involve sorting or maintaining the points in a manner such that they can easily be paired to form these lines, which can add unnecessary computational steps.

In summary, while you certainly could use the set of points from the Midpoint circle algorithm to aid in filling the circle, directly drawing horizontal lines across the circle for each y-value is generally a more direct and efficient approach. It simplifies the logic and can take better advantage of the graphics library's capabilities for drawing lines.

Yes, `decisionOver2` in the provided pseudocode serves the same purpose as `error` in your snippet. Both variables are used in an implementation of the Midpoint Circle Algorithm (also known as the Bresenham's Circle Algorithm) to decide whether to move diagonally or vertically when rasterizing the circle. The name `decisionOver2` implies that the value of the decision variable is divided by two, which can help simplify calculations and avoid floating-point arithmetic by using integers only.

In the Midpoint Circle Algorithm, the `error` or `decisionOver2` variable represents the difference between the ideal circle radius and the actual drawn radius at a given point. It helps to determine when it's necessary to decrease the x-coordinate to maintain the shape of the circle.

The decision variable typically starts at `1 - radius`, and the algorithm checks if the error is less than or equal to zero, which would mean that the y-coordinate must be incremented (moving vertically). If the error becomes positive (greater than zero), it is time to decrement the x-coordinate as well (moving diagonally), in order to keep the plotted points as close to the circle's circumference as possible.

I'll note that the typical increment and decrement operations on `error` or `decisionOver2` are designed to prevent going outside the circle's circumference. The exact calculations you use to adjust the decision variable might differ slightly between different versions of the Midpoint Circle Algorithm, but the core idea remains the same.

Here's a closer look at your snippet:

- `error` starts at `tx - diameter` which is in alignment with the initial decision condition required.
- When `error <= 0`, you increment `y` and add `ty` to the error (because `y` has changed).
- When `error > 0`, you decrement `x`, add `tx` to `error`, and also consider the change in the diameter (because `x` has changed).

Avoiding floating-point operations is significant because integer calculations are more efficient in terms of both processing time and precision. Using `int` also ensures that there is no rounding error that could lead to inaccuracies in the rasterization of the circle.
No I want it to make it simpler without creating new classes or anything new, is there a way to do it with these requirements?
but the line restoreColor();  is written twice too
Nevermind, I thought there was a similar keyword like finally in Python, I think I will just write the same line twice, at least it doesn't add that much complexity as the methods you proposed

OK perfect, now continuing with the exceptions, If I have a MethodA that calls a MethodB which calls a MethodC which throws an exception, do I have to rethrow in every step if I want to watch it in the MethodA for example?
I see interesting, but where do you define this DrawFunction in this part template <typename DrawFunction> or it is a name of the template?
